coredata.dc:title: Rapidly and simply detecting Cr (VI) in aqueous media via a diketopyrrolopyrrole-based chemosensor with both high selectivity and low LOD
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Gholami, Mahnaz D.
Alzubaidi, Fatimah M.
Liu, Qian
Izake, Emad L.
Sonar, Prashant
coredata.dc:description:
Background The high toxicity of hexavalent chromium [Cr (VI)] could not only cause harmful effects on humans, including carcinogenicity, respiratory issues, genetic damage, and skin irritation, but also contaminate drinking water sources, aquatic ecosystems, and soil, impairing the reproductive capacity, growth, and survival of organisms. Due to these harmful effects, detecting toxic Cr (VI) is of great significance. However, the rapid, simple, and efficient detection at a low Cr (VI) concentration is extremely challenging, especially in an acidic condition (existing as HCrO4 −) due to its low adsorption free energy.
Results A diketopyrrolopyrrole-based small molecule (DPPT-PhSMe) is designed and characterized to act as a chemosensor, which allows a high selectivity to Cr (VI) at an acidic condition with a low limit of detection to 10−8 M that is two orders of magnitude lower than the cut of limit (1 μM) recommended by World Health Organization (WHO). Mechanism study indicates that the rich sulfur atoms enhance the affinity to HCrO4 −. Combining with favorable features of diketopyrrolopyrrole, DPPT-PhSMe not only allows dual-mode detection (colorimetric and spectroscopic) to Cr (VI), but also enables disposable paper-based sensor for naked-eye detection to Cr (VI) from fully aqueous media. The investigation of DPPT-PhSMe chemosensor for the quantification of Cr (VI) in real life samples demonstrates a high reliability and accuracy with an average percentage recovery of 102.1 % ± 4 (n = 3).
Significance DPPT-PhSMe represents the first diketopyrrolopyrrole-derived chemosensor for efficient detection to toxic Cr (VI), not only providing a targeted solution to the bottleneck of Cr (VI) detection in acidic conditions (existing as HCrO4 −) caused by its low adsorption free energy, but also opening a new scenario for simple, selective, and efficient Cr (VI) detection with conjugated dye molecules.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Examples of DPP chemosensors for various anions/cations detection with their proposed mechanisms. The current work is the first DPP example to detect toxic Cr (VI).

(a) Synthetic route to DPPT-PhSMe. (b) TGA and (c) DSC thermograms of DPPT-PhSMe. (d) Absorption spectra of DPPT-PhSMe (5 μM) in chloroform and as-cast film. (e) PESA measurement and (f) simulated backbone geometry and energy levels of DPPT-PhSMe.

(a) Absorption spectra of DPPT-PhSMe (2.5 μM) in THF:H2O (1:1) upon addition of different cations (10 eq) at pH = 5. (b) Fluorescence spectra of DPPT-PhSMe (0.5 μM) in THF:H2O (1:1) upon addition of different cations (10 eq) at pH = 5. Images of DPPT-PhSMe solution upon addition of different cations under (c) visible and (d) UV light.

(a) The UV–Vis absorbance changes of DPPT-PhSMe (5 μM) at 613 nm in the presence of Cr (VI) (20 μM) at pH = 5 in 40 min. (b) The intensity variations of UV–Vis spectrum at 613 nm of DPPT-PhSMe (5 μM, in THF:H2O = 1:1) upon the addition of different concentrations of Cr (VI) at pH = 5 (the inset shows the color change of DPPT-PhSMe after addition of Cr (VI)). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

The linear relationships (a–b) between the UV–Vis absorption of DPPT-PhSMe at 613 nm (THF:H2O = 1:1) and the Cr (VI) concentration in the working range from 0.1 to 50 μM at pH = 5, and (c–d) between the fluorescence emission intensity of DPPT-PhSMe at 634 nm (λ ex = 571 nm) and the Cr (VI) concentration in the working range from 0.01 to 5 μM at pH = 5.

(a) Absorption spectra of spiked tap water with Cr (VI) concentrations of 10 and 20 μM in the presence of DPPT-PhSMe chemosensor (5 μM) in THF:H2O (1:1) at pH = 5. (b) Fluorescence spectra of spiked tap water with Cr (VI) concentrations of 0.1 and 2 μM in the presence of DPPT-PhSMe chemosensor (0.5 μM) in THF:H2O (1:1) at pH = 5. (c) The color change of paper-based DPPT-PhSMe sensor upon exposure to Cr (VI) ions in aqueous medium. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Proposed mechanism upon addition of Cr (VI) to DPPT-PhSMe chemosensor.
Optical properties, HOMO and LUMO level of DPPT-PhSMe.

head.ce:title: Rapidly and simply detecting Cr (VI) in aqueous media via a diketopyrrolopyrrole-based chemosensor with both high selectivity and low LOD

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The high toxicity of hexavalent chromium [Cr (VI)] could not only cause harmful effects on humans, including carcinogenicity, respiratory issues, genetic damage, and skin irritation, but also contaminate drinking water sources, aquatic ecosystems, and soil, impairing the reproductive capacity, growth, and survival of organisms. Due to these harmful effects, detecting toxic Cr (VI) is of great significance. However, the rapid, simple, and efficient detection at a low Cr (VI) concentration is extremely challenging, especially in an acidic condition (existing as HCrO4 −) due to its low adsorption free energy.

A diketopyrrolopyrrole-based small molecule (DPPT-PhSMe) is designed and characterized to act as a chemosensor, which allows a high selectivity to Cr (VI) at an acidic condition with a low limit of detection to 10−8 M that is two orders of magnitude lower than the cut of limit (1 μM) recommended by World Health Organization (WHO). Mechanism study indicates that the rich sulfur atoms enhance the affinity to HCrO4 −. Combining with favorable features of diketopyrrolopyrrole, DPPT-PhSMe not only allows dual-mode detection (colorimetric and spectroscopic) to Cr (VI), but also enables disposable paper-based sensor for naked-eye detection to Cr (VI) from fully aqueous media. The investigation of DPPT-PhSMe chemosensor for the quantification of Cr (VI) in real life samples demonstrates a high reliability and accuracy with an average percentage recovery of 102.1 % ± 4 (n = 3).

DPPT-PhSMe represents the first diketopyrrolopyrrole-derived chemosensor for efficient detection to toxic Cr (VI), not only providing a targeted solution to the bottleneck of Cr (VI) detection in acidic conditions (existing as HCrO4 −) caused by its low adsorption free energy, but also opening a new scenario for simple, selective, and efficient Cr (VI) detection with conjugated dye molecules.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hexavalent chromium [Cr (VI)] is highly toxic to cause harmful effects on human health and the environment, which has been categorized as Class I carcinogens by WHO [1]. Human health effects caused by toxic Cr (VI) mainly include carcinogenicity (such as lung cancer, nasal and sinus cancer, and gastrointestinal cancer), respiratory issues (such as asthma, bronchitis, lung irritation, and aggravated existing respiratory conditions caused by inhalation of Cr (VI)), genetic damage (such as DNA mutations, chromosomal aberrations, heritable genetic disorders, and birth defects), and skin irritation (such as rashes, dermatitis, allergies and ulcers) [2–4]. Moreover, improper disposal of Cr (VI) can contaminate drinking water sources, aquatic ecosystems, and soil, impairing the reproductive capacity, growth and survival of a wide range of organisms including aquatic organisms, plants, and soil microorganisms [5]. Due to these harmful effects, monitoring and minimizing exposure to Cr (VI) is crucial to prevent its detrimental impacts.

There have been some physical techniques used for Cr (VI) sensing, while these methods often require high operation skills, heavy & expensive instruments, and a long time for detection [6–10]. Chemosensor is one of the efficient methods to address these issues due to its desirable features like being simple (or direct), cheap, and detectable to various ions in aqueous medium with naked eyes [11–14]. However, to the best of our knowledge, effective chemosensors for chromium ions detection mostly target to Cr (III), leaving a great challenge to Cr (VI) [15]. The reported chemosensors for Cr (VI) detection are mostly heavy metal complex [16–23], carbon dots [24–29], and metal-organic framework (MOF) [30,31], which to some extents are inherently toxic and unstable. For example, heavy metals including Ru [23], La [31], Zn [32], Cr [20], Cd [19], Sr [17], and Au [16] have been applied to develop chemosensors for Cr (VI) detection. Moreover, certain designed chemosensors can not only detect Cr (VI), but also simultaneously sensitive to other analytes, include Cr (III) [22], Fe (III) [21,31], Cu (II) [17], Hg (II) [32], ascorbic acid [33], antibiotics [34] and TNP [22]. To avoid these issues, organic conjugated dyes represent a promising class of chemosensors because it combines both the advantages of colorimetric and spectroscopic methods [35]. Despite that, chemosensors for Cr (VI) detection with organic conjugated dyes were rarely reported. Zhang et al. reported a rhodamine derivative (Cr6P1, structure shown in Supporting Information), in which the spirocyclic ring could be opened due to the strong oxidation of Cr (VI) to inhibit the excited-state intramolecular proton transfer [36]. In this way, naked-eye color change and fluorescence quenching were clearly observed, delivering a selective detection to Cr (VI) with a low LOD of 13 nM [36]. Introducing SH groups to a fluorescent carbazole derivative, delivering a new chemosensor ECBAD (structure shown in Supporting Information), which selectively generates electrostatic/hydrogen-bond interactions with Cr (VI) among 17 anions. This enables a significant fluorescence quenching excited at 365 nm, contributing to a LOD of 0.1 μM to Cr (VI) [37]. These reports mainly detect CrO4 2− or Cr2O7 2−. However, at pH < 6.5 and at a trace-level concentration, Cr (VI) exists as HCrO4 − which has a low adsorption free energy making it difficult to be effectively detected [38].

Despite various reports about DPP sensors for diverse anions/cations detection, selectively sensing toxic Cr (VI) with DPP materials hasn't been achieved. It was extensively studied that thiol compounds, for example cysteine and glutathione, can form thiolate complexes with Cr (VI) [50,51]. Additionally, upon exposure to Cr (VI), thiol species form Cr (VI)-thioester complexes prior to reducing Cr (VI) to Cr (III) with themselves being oxidized to disulfides, which was characterised by HPLC, NMR, and UV–Vis techniques [52,53]. Tan et al. made colorimetric sensors with dithiothreitol modified gold nanoparticles (DTT-AuNPs) to selectively detect Cr (VI), which can be attributed to the formation of Cr (VI)-DTT-AuNPs complexes, subsequently generating disulfides in between DTT-AuNPs, facilitating the aggregation of DTT-AuNPs for visible color changing [54–56]. Combining the favorable properties of DPP in sensing mentioned above, and utilizing the specific intermediates formed between Cr (VI) and thiol compounds, it is highly promising to develop and study DPP-based chemosensors for Cr (VI) sensing.

Herein, we report a novel and simple molecule via end-capping DPP with electron donating thioanisole, DPPT-PhSMe, being able to act as a highly efficient chemosensor for Cr (VI) through colorimetric, absorbance, and fluorescence modes. This simply sensor has a high selectivity to Cr (VI) in acidic condition among more than 10 cations with significant visible color quenching, measurable absorption and fluorescent reduction. The LOD of fluorescent sensing is as low as 10−8 M that is two orders of magnitude lower than the cut of limit Cr (VI) concentration recommended by WHO, being highly promising for low-level Cr (VI) sensing. Such a promising sensing capability is ascribed to the rich sulfur atoms to form four-fold additions with Cr (VI), combined with the properties of DPP unit for a visibly observed color change and strong measurable absorption/fluorescence variation. The high selectivity and low LOD of DPP-PhSMe sensor towards Cr (VI) also enable its practical application for quantitative detection in real life samples.

Tetrahydrofuran, anhydrous toluene, ethyl acetate, hexane, silica gel, methanol, chloroform, acetonitrile, fluorescein, Hg(NO3)2 • H2O, NaCl, FeSO4 • 7H2O, Ni(NO3)2 • 6H2O, (CH3COO)2Mn • 4H2O, CrCl3(III) • 6H2O, ZnSO4 • 7H2O, Cd(NO3)2 • 4H2O, Pb(NO3)2, CrO3, KCl, MgCl2, were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Deionised (DI) water was utilised to prepare aqueous solutions.

Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra were performed using an Arthur II FT-IR spectrometer (wavelength range 400–4000 cm−1) from Bruker (Germany). A 600 MHz NMR spectrometer (Bruker, UK) was used to carry out 1H NMR in deuterated chloroform and tetramethyl silane (internal standard). Mass spectra were acquired on a high-resolution Orbitrap Elite (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Germany) equipped with an atmospheric pressure chemical ionisation source operating in the positive ion mode. Mass resolving power was set to 120,000 (at m/z 400). Sample was dissolved in THF and diluted in THF:MeOH (3:2, v/v) and infused into the ion source at 10 μL/min. Cary 60 UV–Vis Spectrophotometer and Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA) were utilised for recording the absorption spectra and fluorescence emission spectra, respectively. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) techniques were performed using a simultaneous thermal analyser (STA 449F3 Jupiter, Netzsch, Germany).

Under argon protection, in a 100 mL Schlenk flask, C12-DPPT-Br (794.79 mg, 1.0 mmol), tributyl(4-(methylthio)phenyl)stannane (1.24 g, 3.0 mmol), Pd2(dba)3 (45.79 mg, 50 μmol), and P(o-tol)3 (121.75 mg, 0.4 mmol) are mixed in 30 mL anhydrous toluene. The reaction mixture is stirred evenly and heated up to 110 °C for around 8 h. After cooling down to 25 °C, the solvent is removed and the obtained residue is purified with silicon column with hexane:ethyl acetate (5:1 vol%) to achieve pure DPPT-PhSMe (550 mg, yield = 62.40 %). 1H NMR (600 MHz, CDCl3) δ (ppm) 8.97 (s, 2H), 7.59 (d, J = 4.8 Hz, 4H), 7.43 (d, J = 4.2 Hz, 2H), 7.30 (s, 4H), 4.12 (t, J = 7.5 Hz, 4H), 2.55 (s, 6H), 1.85–1.77 (m, 4H), 1.51–1.46 (m, 4H), 1.43–1.38 (m, 4H), 1.35–1.25 (m, 28H), 0.90 (t, J = 6.9 Hz, 6H). HRMS: measured m/z 881.4253 (expected 881.4236 for [C52H68N2O2S4 +H]+). Δ = 1.9 ppm.

Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) were utilised to study the thermal behavior of DPPT-PhSMe using a simultaneous thermal analyser (STA 449F3 Jupiter, Netzsch, Germany). For TGA measurement, DPPT-PhSMe is heated from 20 °C to 800 °C with a rate of 10 °C min−1 under a nitrogen atmosphere. For DSC measurement, upon the heating and cooling cycle, DPPT-PhSMe is heated from 25 °C to 300 °C with a rate of 10 °C min−1 and then cooled down to 25 °C with a rate of 5 °C min−1 under a nitrogen atmosphere.

Stock solutions of DPPT-PhSMe (0.1 mM) in THF and various cations (1 mM) include CrO3, Hg(NO3)2, NaCl, FeSO4, Ni(NO3)2, (CH3COO)2Mn, CrCl3(III), ZnSO4, Cd(NO3)2, Pb(NO3)2, KCl, and MgCl2 in DI water were prepared. Sensing capability of DPPT-PhSMe towards different cations were studied through mixing 250 μL of DPPT-PhSMe (0.1 mM) in THF with 250 μL of various cation solution (1 mM) to the final volume of 10 mL (diluted with THF) and pH was adjusted to 5 using HCl. The adsorption spectra of mixtures were recorded using the UV–Vis spectrometer (wavelength: 300–800 nm). To explore fluorescence sensing, 25 μL of the DPPT-PhSMe (0.1 mM) in THF was mixed with 25 μL of the cation solutions (1 mM) to the final volume of 5 mL (diluted with THF) and pH was adjusted to 5 using HCl. Fluorescence emissions were acquired using excitation wavelength of 571 nm (wavelength range 572–800 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of DPPT-PhSMe and Cr (VI) complex by FT-IR
ce:section.ce:para: Solutions of DPPT-PhSMe (0.1 M) and CrO3 (1 M) were prepared in THF and water, respectively. 100 μL of DPPT-PhSMe was mixed with 4 μL of Cr (VI) to sit for 20 min for full reaction. Then, FT-IR spectra of resulting mixture was measured by attenuated total reflection Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR FT-IR).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of stoichiometry of the DPPT-PhSMe - Cr (VI)
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the stoichiometry between DPPT-PhSMe and Cr (VI), UV–Vis spectroscopic titration was utilised. Various concentrations of Cr (VI) (0.1–20 molar equivalents) were added into DPPT-PhSMe solution (with a fixed concentration of 5 × 10−6 M). The absorption intensity of DPPT-PhSMe/Cr (VI) mixture at 613 nm was acquired to plot against the molar ratio of Cr (VI) to DPPT-PhSMe.

For quantification of Cr (VI) in water with absorption spectra, 250 μL of DPPT-PhSMe (0.1 mM) were mixed with different volumes of Cr solution (1 mM), which was diluted with THF to final volume of 5 mL (pH = 5). The DPPT-PhSMe concentration was kept constant (5 μM) and the final Cr (VI) concentration varied from 0.1 μM to 50 μM. From the recorded absorption spectra, the maximum absorption (λ max = 613 nm) was plotted versus Cr (VI) concentrations. To determine Cr (VI) using fluorescence spectroscopy, DPPT-PhSMe concentration was fixed at 0.5 μM and the concentration of Cr (VI) was changed from 0.01 μM to 5 μM (pH = 5). The intensity at 634 nm was plotted against concentrations of Cr (VI).

The synthesis of DPPT-PhSMe is shown in Fig. 2 a and its chemical structure was characterised by NMR (Fig. S1) and HRMS (Fig. S2) as described in Supporting Information. The thermal properties of DPPT-PhSMe were studied by utilizing TGA and DSC techniques. The acquired thermograms are given in Fig. 2b. The decomposition temperature (T d, at 5 % weight loss) of DPPT-PhSMe is estimated to be 371 °C, indicating its high thermal stability that allows various annealing strategies to optimize performance upon being applied in potential electronic devices. The DSC analysis delivers strong and sharp peaks upon heating and cooling processes, demonstrating a high crystallinity of DPPT-PhSMe. The melting and crystalline peaks are located at 55/214 °C and 46/210 °C, respectively. The low temperature melting and crystalline peaks are owing to the transition of flexible side chains and the high temperature peaks are attributed to the transition of conjugated backbone (Fig. 2c).

As seen from the ultraviolet visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra of DPPT-PhSMe in Fig. 2d, and the summarized data in Table 1 , the typical dual bands, Band I (300–500 nm) & Band II (500–750 nm), are ascribed to π-π* transition of the conjugated backbone and intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) effect from donor (thioanisole) to acceptor (DPP core) units, respectively. A shoulder peak is observed in solution, indicating that π stacking appears even in dilute solution. As thin film, various peaks with broad and red-shifted curves are observed, indicating that the molecular stacking is enhanced and different types of π stacking orientations are formed. The onset of thin film is located at around 704 nm, representing a bandgap of 1.76 eV. Photoelectron yield spectroscopy in air (PESA) was performed to estimate the HOMO level. Combined with the HOMO level of −5.28 eV determined by the PESA measurement as shown in Fig. 2e, the LUMO level of DPPT-PhSMe is calculated to be −3.52 eV. Density functional theory (DFT) was utilised to investigate the backbone geometry, energy levels, and molecular orbital distributions. As shown in Fig. 2f, DPPT-PhSMe has a high backbone planarity, being favorable for multi-mode molecular stacking, being consistent with the absorption spectrum in film. The simulated HOMO and LUMO levels are −5.20/-3.05 eV, which is slightly higher but overall consistent with experimental results. The simulated gap is higher than that of measured optical bandgap, which is resulted from the effect of vibrational broadening upon measuring, while the simulated results are based on fixed equilibrium geometry [57]. Both the HOMO and LUMO are observed to delocalize along the entire backbone, which is a positive signal for chemical sensing as the reaction with detected objects could affect the electron cloud density thus resulting in a significant variation in properties for reliable sensing capability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and fluorescence sensing of Cr (VI) by DPPT-PhSMe
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the selectivity of DPPT-PhSMe dye to Cr (VI), the UV–Vis absorption spectra of DPPT-PhSMe solutions in THF:H2O (1:1) in presence of various cations were recorded (Fig. 3 a). A distinct visible color change from purple to colorless was observed only towards addition of Cr (VI). The addition of other cations, including mercury nitrate (Hg2+), sodium chloride (Na+), iron sulphate (Fe2+), nickel nitrate (Ni2+), manganese acetate (Mn2+), chromium chloride (Cr3+), zinc sulphate (Zn2+), cadmium nitrate (Cd2+), lead nitrate (Pb2+), potassium chloride (K+), magnesium chloride (Mg2+), with their associated anions, did not cause any color change of the DPPT-PhSMe dye solution (Fig. 3c). Furthermore, the maximum absorption band of DPPT-PhSMe at 571 and 613 nm experienced a significant reduction in presence of Cr (VI). By contrast, no significant changes in the maximum absorption band were obtained upon the addition of other cations, indicating the high selectivity of DPPT-PhSMe dye towards Cr (VI) (Fig. 3a).

DPPT-PhSMe is a strongly fluorescent dye with maximum emission band at 634 nm (λ ex = 571 nm) with a photoluminescence quantum yield (Φ F) of 24 % in THF solvent using fluorescein in EtOH as standard (Φ F = 0.79). To verify the selectivity of fluorescent response of DPPT-PhSMe towards Cr (VI), 10 equivalents of various cations including Pb2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, K+, Na+, Cr3+, Cd2+, Zn2+ and Hg2+ were added to the dye solution in THF:H2O (1:1). The fluorescence of the dye was quenched in presence of Cr (VI), while in contrast, no significant changes in the emission intensity of DPPT-PhSMe at 634 nm were observed upon the addition of other cations/anions (Fig. 3b and d). The selectivity of DPPT-PhSMe sensor was qualitatively confirmed by screening an aqueous mixture of Pb2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, K+, Na+, Cr3+, Cd2+, Zn2+ and Hg2+ (final concentration of each ion = 20 μM) in the absence (negative control sample) and presence of Cr (VI) ion (final concentration = 2 μM, positive control sample). As indicated by Fig. S3, the presence of the Cr (VI) ion caused a decolorization of the sensor. Therefore, DPPT-PhSMe could be utilised as a new chemosensor for selective detection of Cr (VI) via the colorimetric and fluorescence dual mode.

The thiol groups in DPPT-PhSMe molecular structure play a key role in the Cr (VI) detection. Cr (VI) existed as Cr2O7 2−, HCrO4 −, and CrO4 2− in solution at optimum pH. Cr (VI) was presented in the HCrO4 − form at pH < 6.5 from the hydrolysis of dichromate ion, which has a low adsorption free energy making it hard to be detected [58–60]. As indicated in Scheme 1
, HCrO4 − can be adsorbed by DPPT-PhSMe through the high affinity of sulfur atom to chromate anion. The binding of HCrO4 − to DPPT-PhSMe was also monitored by FT-IR. The FT-IR spectrum of DPPT-PhSMe after addition of HCrO4 − showed a broad band at 2180 and 814 cm−1 that can be assigned to C–S stretching vibration ( Fig. S4 ). Additionally, there is a new broad peak at 1950 cm−1 that can be attributed to the complex formation between the DPPT-PhSMe molecule and the HCrO4 − ion and a C–O vibration mode within the formed complex. These peaks verify the proposed adsorption mechanism of HCrO4 − as illustrated in Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimum response time for sensing Cr (VI) by DPPT-PhSMe
ce:section.ce:para: To study the time effect of Cr (VI) determination via DPPT-PhSMe dye, an aliquot of Cr (VI) solution was added into the DPPT-PhSMe prob solution to record the absorbance at 613 nm within 40 min. As indicated in Fig. 4 a, the absorption intensity of DPPT-PhSMe at 613 nm decreased gradually towards the addition of Cr (VI). After 20 min, the absorbance changes of the DPPT-PhSMe at 613 nm became negligible. Therefore, the optimum time for a complete reaction between DPPT-PhSMe dye and Cr (VI) was determined to be 20 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis titration of DPPT-PhSMe chemosensor towards Cr (VI)
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the stoichiometry of the reaction between DPPT-PhSMe chemosensor and the Cr (VI), the DPPT-PhSMe dye concentration was constant at 5 μM while the Cr (VI) concentrations varied from 0 to 20 molar equivalents. Upon mixing Cr (VI) with DPPT-PhSMe, the mixture was acetic with a pH value of 5. By recording the maximum absorption at 613 nm after adding different concentrations of Cr (VI), as exhibited in Fig. 4b, the stoichiometry between the DPPT-PhSMe chemosensor and Cr (VI) was estimated to be 1:4 (DPPT-PhSMe:Cr (VI)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of Cr (VI) using UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscope
ce:section.ce:para: To quantify the Cr (VI) in water, the maximum absorption band of DPPT-PhSMe at 613 nm in presence of various concentrations of Cr (VI) was recorded by UV–Vis spectroscopy (Fig. 5 a). A linear relationship between the absorbance intensity at 613 nm and the concentrations of Cr (VI) varying from 1 × 10−7 M to 50 × 10−6 M (0.1–50 μM) was observed, which was formulated (Fig. 5b) to be y = −0.2727x + 0.5323 (R2 = 0.9819). Similarly, upon utilizing fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 5c) to quantify the Cr (VI) in aqueous solution by DPPT-PhSMe, the linear relationship between the emission intensity at 634 nm and the Cr (VI) concentrations varying from 1 × 10−8 M to 5 × 10−6 M (0.01–5 μM) was delivered to be y = −109.06x + 117.57 (R2 = 0.9912) (Fig. 5d). Comparing with UV–Vis method, a one order of magnitude lower LOD for the quantification of Cr (VI) by fluorescence method was derived to be 1 × 10−8 M (0.01 μΜ). Therefore, DPPT-PhSMe can determine Cr (VI) down to 0.01 μΜ, which is two orders of magnitude lower than the permissible cut of limit (1 μΜ) recommended by the World Health Organization (WHO) and the United States Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA) [59]. Additionally, the LOD of DPPT-PhSMe for Cr (VI) detection is also among the lowest values compared with reported chemosensors as present in Table S1 in the supporting information, including metal complex, carbon dots, and other organic conjugated molecules.

To investigate the potential of the DPPT-PhSMe chemosensor for the quantification of Cr (VI) in real life samples, spiked tap water with Cr (VI) to a concentration of 1 × 10−4 M (100 μM) was prepared. Various volumes of spiked tape water were mixed with THF to final concentrations of Cr (VI) including 0.1, 2, 10 and 20 μM. UV–Vis spectroscopy was utilised for the quantification of 10 and 20 μM of Cr (VI) as shown in Fig. 6 a. The regression equation in Fig. 5b indicated that the determined concentrations of Cr (VI) were 9.95 and 21 μM, respectively. The intensity of the fluorescence emission at 634 nm of spiked tap water with Cr (VI) concentrations of 0.1 and 2 μM were recorded by fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 6b). The determined concentrations of Cr (VI) in the spiked samples were found to be 0.095 and 2.04 μM using the regression equation in Fig. 5d. The average percentage recovery was 102.1 % ± 4 (n = 3). These results clearly demonstrate the reliability and accuracy of quantitative analysis of the newly developed DPPT-PhSMe-based sensor for toxic Cr (VI) detection from both colorimetric and spectroscopic dual modes in real life samples, indicating its huge potential for applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Paper-based DPPT-PhSMe chemosensor for simple detection of Cr (VI)
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the prospective of newly developed DPPT-PhSMe sensor for the on-site detection of Cr (VI), 5 μM of the dye solution (in THF) was loaded onto a filter paper strip and allowed to dry at room temperature in the fume cupboard. The dye-loaded paper sensor was then immersed in an aqueous Cr (VI) solution (concentration = 30 μM) for 20 min, making DPPT-PhSMe a full reaction with Cr (VI). Fig. 6c shows the color change of the filter paper from purple to colorless after exposure to the Cr (VI) ions. This result confirms that the DPPT-PhSMe can be utilised to make disposable paper sensor, which allows rapid detection of Cr (VI) from fully aqueous media.

In summary, a novel diketopyrrolopyrrole-based molecule (DPPT-PhSMe) was designed and synthesised by attaching thioanisole as terminal groups. The rich sulfur atoms provide a targeted solution to the bottleneck in HCrO4 − sensing (the form of toxic Cr (VI) in acidic condition) caused by its low adsorption free energy. Combined with the strong donor-acceptor (thioanisole-diketopyrrolopyrrole) intramolecular charge transfer and fluorescent property, DPPT-PhSMe represents the first example for Cr (VI) sensing based on the promising diketopyrrolopyrrole dye unit. More promisingly, DPPT-PhSMe shows colorimetric and spectroscopic dual-mode sensing for rapid and simple detection of Cr (VI) with a specifically high selectivity and low LOD (as low as 10−8 M). This sensing property is also successfully applied for quantitative detection of Cr (VI) in real life samples and as a disposable paper sensor for rapid naked-eye detection of Cr (VI) from fully aqueous media, demonstrating its potential practicability in real world. Our work not only reports the first diketopyrrolopyrrole-based sensor for toxic Cr (VI) detection, but also provides a new scenario that rational molecular design by simply modifying the DPP unit or selecting other promising building blocks to enhance the adsorption with HCrO4 − and to simultaneously keep remarkable optical features is an effective strategy to develop reliable sensors, especially targeting to the bottlenecks in rapid, simple and selective detection of toxic Cr (VI).
